summary:
Martin Luther's 95 Theses (October 31, 1517 AD) — formally titled 'Disputation on the Power and Efficacy of Indulgences' — were a scholarly challenge to the Catholic Church's sale of indulgences (certificates purportedly reducing time in purgatory) that, amplified by the [[event:3834|printing press]], triggered the [[event:102|Protestant Reformation]] and permanently fractured Western Christianity. Luther, an Augustinian friar and professor of theology at Wittenberg, was outraged by the preaching of Johann Tetzel, a Dominican friar selling indulgences whose funds were partly destined for the rebuilding of St. Peter's Basilica in Rome — Tetzel's reported sales pitch ('When the coin in the coffer rings, the soul from purgatory springs') exemplified the commercial corruption Luther attacked. He composed 95 academic theses for debate, reportedly nailing them to the door of the Castle Church in Wittenberg (the standard procedure for proposing scholarly debate).

The 95 Theses spread across Germany with extraordinary speed, thanks entirely to the printing press: within two weeks they had been printed and distributed throughout Germany; within two months they were known across Europe — a demonstration of the new medium's transformative power for religious communication. Luther had not initially intended a break with Rome; his early position was that the Pope, if he knew what was being done in his name, would surely stop it. But under papal pressure to recant (and the realization that the theological contradictions could not be resolved within Catholic orthodoxy), Luther's positions escalated: at the Leipzig Debate (1519) he publicly defended Jan Hus (burned for heresy a century earlier); at the [[event:2495|Diet of Worms]] (1521) he declared 'Here I stand, I can do no other.' His excommunication and the Edict of Worms made him an outlaw, but the movement he had ignited could no longer be stopped.

read_more_links.title: Johann Tetzel indulgence selling St Peter's scandal
Printing press 95 Theses spread Germany Europe
